title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Frankie learns from Mrs. Baskinton-French that Tommy recently suffered a fall from a swing when a rotten branch broke, though he has since recovered. Frankie observes Henry Baskinton-French during tea and dinner, noting his erratic behavior; he oscillates between nervous twitching and dazed states to sharp, sarcastic outbursts, followed by an unnaturally brilliant and rejuvenated demeanor at dinner. Frankie notices his peculiar, scary eyes and suspects he may be an addict, though she initially doubts his involvement in the murder because his brother was in Machabert when the crime occurred.

The following afternoon, Roger Baskin-French arrives for tea. He is a tall, thin man with deep blue eyes who brings a Hornby train for Tommy. Frankie’s impression of Roger as a likable man leads her to conclude that her and Bobby's initial theory—that Roger pushed Prichard off the cliff—is incorrect. However, she remains puzzled by the identity of the murderer and the source of the morphine placed in Bobby's beer.

The next day, while playing tennis, Roger confides in Frankie that he is certain Henry is using drugs, likely morphine, to treat insomnia. He notes that Henry’s pupils are as small as needle tips and observes that Henry becomes neurotic when his supply runs low, only to transform after receiving a six o'clock parcel. Roger admits he is sharing this information because he does not know how to face Sylvia. Frankie reflects on the possibility of a drug smuggling gang but remains wary, reminding herself that charming individuals can also be murderers.
